title: The Beatitudes Part 4
scripture_text: ‘’Now when Jesus saw the crowds, he went up on a mountainside and sat down. His disciples came to him, and he began to teach them.
devotional:
He said: “Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted. Blessed are the meek for they will inherit the earth. Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled. Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God. Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called children of God. Blessed are those who are persecuted because of righteousness, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you and falsely say all kinds of evil against you because of me. Rejoice and be glad, because great is your reward in heaven, for in the same way they persecuted the prophets who were before you.’’

"Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God."

The Pharisees were not pure in heart. "Purity of heart" is not merely the avoidance of "impure thoughts" (e.g. sexual fantasies), but refers to the single-minded devotion to God.

The word "pure" gives us the idea of "cleansing" which is the central idea in the Greek word katharos from which we get our word, "catharsis" - meaning to "cleanse the emotions or spirit. This 'pure in heart' could be described as being whole-heartedly committed to God and is the consequence of mourning. Refusal to deal with the issues of sin and pain will distract us from being whole-hearted in our walk with God. Paul taught this same principle in Philippians 3:1-14. He had to leave behind the past so he could be wholeheartedly devoted to God. When we refuse to be honest with ourselves and deal with the issues that are going on in our hearts, then we will be incapable of being whole-hearted in our commitment to God. When we bury our pain, we bury our commitment to God in the process.

"Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called sons of God."

The Pharisees did not understand the meaning of real peace. For a Pharisee, peace meant having the ability to control things or people, so their expectations and needs would be met. Jesus said, "Blessed are the peacemakers for they will be called the sons of God." It is important to notice that Jesus is not talking about peacekeepers, but peacemakers.

So what is a peacemaker?

To help us answer this question, it would be helpful to say what a peacemaker is not. A peacemaker is not someone who tries to keep the peace at all costs; they are not someone who buries issues, hoping that they will go away, they would never sweep things under the carpet. In fact a peacemaker may be viewed as a troublemaker, why? Because they will insist that an issue will be brought out into the open, so it can be dealt with properly.

The ultimate expression of peacemaking is Jesus' death on the cross. "For God was pleased to have all his fullness dwell in him, and through him to reconcile to himself all things, whether things on earth or things in heaven, by making peace through his blood, shed on the cross." Colossians 1:19-20. In other words, peacemaking is very demanding and painful. This is not a licence for us to go around and always being in people's face or on their cases. To be a peacemaker you must also be poor in spirit, mournful over your own failure, gentle, etc. If we are not pure in heart, then we are in need of the help of a peacemaker for a lack of peace within ourselves, and with others, is an indication that we are not pure in heart.

For some people like myself, this is the most scary and demanding beatitude, because of our dislike of confrontation. But if we are going to be known as sons of God, then we must be willing to be involved in peacemaking, or the "ministry of reconciliation," to use Paul's phrase.
prayer: Father, help me to have the heart of a peacemaker.
html:
The Beatitudes Part 4

Study Text: Matthew 5:1-12
‘’Now when Jesus saw the crowds, he went up on a mountainside and sat down. His disciples came to him, and he began to teach them.

He said: “Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted. Blessed are the meek for they will inherit the earth. Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled. Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God. Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called children of God. Blessed are those who are persecuted because of righteousness, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you and falsely say all kinds of evil against you because of me. Rejoice and be glad, because great is your reward in heaven, for in the same way they persecuted the prophets who were before you.’’

"Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God."

The Pharisees were not pure in heart. "Purity of heart" is not merely the avoidance of "impure thoughts" (e.g. sexual fantasies), but refers to the single-minded devotion to God.

The word "pure" gives us the idea of "cleansing" which is the central idea in the Greek word katharos from which we get our word, "catharsis" - meaning to "cleanse the emotions or spirit. This 'pure in heart' could be described as being whole-heartedly committed to God and is the consequence of mourning. Refusal to deal with the issues of sin and pain will distract us from being whole-hearted in our walk with God. Paul taught this same principle in Philippians 3:1-14. He had to leave behind the past so he could be wholeheartedly devoted to God. When we refuse to be honest with ourselves and deal with the issues that are going on in our hearts, then we will be incapable of being whole-hearted in our commitment to God. When we bury our pain, we bury our commitment to God in the process.

"Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called sons of God."

The Pharisees did not understand the meaning of real peace. For a Pharisee, peace meant having the ability to control things or people, so their expectations and needs would be met. Jesus said, "Blessed are the peacemakers for they will be called the sons of God." It is important to notice that Jesus is not talking about peacekeepers, but peacemakers.

So what is a peacemaker?

To help us answer this question, it would be helpful to say what a peacemaker is not. A peacemaker is not someone who tries to keep the peace at all costs; they are not someone who buries issues, hoping that they will go away, they would never sweep things under the carpet. In fact a peacemaker may be viewed as a troublemaker, why? Because they will insist that an issue will be brought out into the open, so it can be dealt with properly.

The ultimate expression of peacemaking is Jesus' death on the cross. "For God was pleased to have all his fullness dwell in him, and through him to reconcile to himself all things, whether things on earth or things in heaven, by making peace through his blood, shed on the cross." Colossians 1:19-20. In other words, peacemaking is very demanding and painful. This is not a licence for us to go around and always being in people's face or on their cases. To be a peacemaker you must also be poor in spirit, mournful over your own failure, gentle, etc. If we are not pure in heart, then we are in need of the help of a peacemaker for a lack of peace within ourselves, and with others, is an indication that we are not pure in heart.

For some people like myself, this is the most scary and demanding beatitude, because of our dislike of confrontation. But if we are going to be known as sons of God, then we must be willing to be involved in peacemaking, or the "ministry of reconciliation," to use Paul's phrase.

Prayer: Father, help me to have the heart of a peacemaker.
Bible in a year reading plan: -Judges 20:12-21:25; Ruth 1:1-2:13
